destination.vibe: The flattest big city in Europe, wrapped in Moorish palaces and orange trees, with 180km of dedicated bike lanes connecting neighborhoods that haven't changed in 500 years — you just have to be on the road before the sun gets serious.

flights.outbound_dates: July–August flexible (Tue/Wed departures cheapest; book 4–6 weeks out)
flights.notes: Connects through Istanbul (IST) — Turkish flies from DTW with good IST connection frequency. Full-service airline with meals and 2 checked bags included. Important: call ahead to confirm bike-as-checked-baggage policy and fees (typically €50–80 each way in a hard case or bike box); book on turkishairlines.com directly for clearest fee disclosure.

flights.outbound_dates: July–August flexible (midweek cheapest; August slightly cheaper than July)
flights.notes: Connects through Madrid Barajas (MAD) — the most logical routing since MAD→SVQ is only 1h15m on Iberia's Puente Aéreo shuttle (flights every 30–60 min). Bike fees: Iberia charges €50–100 each way for bikes in a case; confirm at iberia.com. From MAD to SVQ you could also take the AVE high-speed train (2.5h, €30–60) if the connection time allows — Renfe allows bikes in bike-specific spaces on some trains, so worth checking.

Connects through Amsterdam Schiphol (AMS) — KLM is part of SkyTeam. Short AMS→SVQ leg (~3h). KLM bike policy: bikes accepted as checked baggage at €50–75 each way; must be in a hard case or bike box and deflated/pedals removed. Book at klm.com and add the bike during checkout.

lodging.name: La Banda Rooftop Hostel
lodging.highlights: Well-reviewed hostel steps from the Torre del Oro and Guadalquivir riverfront — the best starting point for the city cycling circuit. Ask at check-in for secure indoor bike storage (policy: confirm before booking). Rooftop terrace with views of the Cathedral tower.

lodging.name: Hotel Palacio Alcázar
lodging.neighborhood: Santa Cruz / Barrio de Santa Cruz
lodging.highlights: Boutique hotel in a 19th-century palace 200m from the Real Alcázar walls, central for city cycling circuits and the Guadalquivir promenade. Confirm secure bike storage when booking — standard at most Seville hotel category; this one has interior parking. 4.3/5 on Booking.

lodging.name: Hotel Corral del Rey
lodging.highlights: A 17th-century merchant's townhouse restored to 13 rooms in the oldest part of the city — four courtyards, original stone, genuinely beautiful. Secure bike storage available. This is the most atmospheric address in Seville and worth it for two nights.

itinerary.overview: Seville is one of the great surprises of European cycling — not because anyone expects it to be bad, but because 180km of separated bike lanes through a city of Moorish palaces, orange trees, and flamenco bars is a combination that doesn't quite make sense until you're riding it at 6:30am with the Cathedral lit gold and nobody else on the road. The terrain is completely flat: the Guadalquivir delta has been filling in for ten thousand years and the result is a city where every elevation change is a bridge. The surrounding countryside is more varied — the Sierra Norte to the north, the Doñana wetlands to the southwest, whitewashed hill towns to the east — and all of it is accessible by bike via greenways and quiet provincial roads. The catch, and it is a real one: July in Seville is genuinely hostile. Temperatures regularly hit 40–43°C by noon. All serious cycling must happen before 9am (ideally departing by 5:45–6am in summer) or after 6:30pm. Local cyclists know this and operate accordingly — the city's cycling infrastructure comes alive at dawn and at dusk, and the middle of the day belongs to shade, cold drinks, and the siesta. Plan the same way and Seville rewards you completely.

days.title: The Great Seville Circuit — 32km city loop at dawn, then monuments on foot
days.morning: Wheels rolling by 6am. The route: start at the Torre del Oro on the Guadalquivir riverside, head south along the river's tree-lined bike boulevard past the Parque de María Luisa to the Plaza de España (the 1929 World's Fair semicircular palace complex — deserted at this hour, extraordinary). Loop north through the Barrio de Santa Cruz, past the Cathedral's south transept, through the Barrio de Triana via the Puente de Isabel II, and north along the Cartuja island's cycling path to the Alamillo Park and bridge. The full circuit is roughly 32km on dedicated infrastructure and takes 2–2.5 hours at a moderate touring pace. The city is a different thing at this hour: light low and orange, the orange trees still fragrant from the night, delivery workers and cleaning crews the only other people moving, the Cathedral tower slowly appearing above rooflines as you approach from the south. Back at the hotel by 8:30am for breakfast and a shower before the heat arrives.
days.afternoon: On foot during the hottest hours. The Real Alcázar (book timed entry online at the gate or via alcazarsevilla.org, €14.50) is one of the great buildings in Europe: a Moorish palace continuously occupied and expanded since the 10th century, with courtyard gardens that stay cool even in July (the water channels were designed for exactly this). The Cathedral next door is the world's largest Gothic church — climb the Giralda tower (the former minaret) for the definitive city view. Between them, two hours passes easily. Afterwards: the Barrio de Santa Cruz on foot for shade, narrow alleys, and a cold fino sherry at a bar.
days.evening: Evening ride: 6:30pm departure for the Guadalquivir riverside south. The Vía Verde de los Colegios follows the river south from the city toward Coria del Río (12km, flat, completely paved) — at dusk the light on the river and Triana's coloured facades is the payoff for surviving the afternoon. Coria del Río has bars on the waterfront. Ride back under the rising moon. Dinner in Triana after 9pm: tapas at one of the old azulejo-tiled bars on Calle Pelay Correa — coquinas (tiny clams, €4 a plate), montaditos, and a cold cerveza.

days.title: Italica greenway + Guadalquivir Marismas — road and gravel day
days.morning: 5:45am departure for the longest day. Route north on the Vía Verde greenway toward Santiponce (12km, paved, flat) — the town of Santiponce contains the Roman city of Italica, founded 206 BC, birthplace of emperors Trajan and Hadrian. The amphitheater is one of the largest in the Roman world (capacity 25,000), open from 9am (€1.50), and at 7am you'll have the surrounding ruins to yourself to circle on the bike. Continue north via quiet provincial roads toward La Algaba and El Garrobo (cumulative ~45km from Seville, 275m elevation — the one genuine climb of the trip, through holm oak and wildflower scrub into the Sierra Norte foothills). The village of Venta del Alto at the high point has a roadside restaurant that opens early for cyclists. Descend back through Salteras and Valencina (archaeological site: megalithic tomb complex dating to 3000 BC, often open on the roadside with no entrance booth) to the city on the dedicated bike lane via Camas.
days.afternoon: Rest, shower, long lunch, siesta. This is mandatory, not optional — two hours of serious riding in Andalusian summer requires recovery. The city's covered mercado (Mercado de Triana, Calle San Jorge) has cooked fish and cold drinks at market stalls.
days.evening: Evening exploration: short social ride through the Macarena neighborhood — the working-class barrio north of the historic center, genuinely non-touristic, with some of the best tapas bars in the city (Bar Europa, Bar Eslava on Calle Eslava, consistently considered one of the best tapas bars in all of Seville). The Basílica de la Macarena houses the city's most beloved Semana Santa float; if the door is open, go in. Ride back south through the old city walls at dusk.

days.title: Via Verde de los Alcores — Alcalá de Guadaíra to Carmona, 60km gravel
days.morning: Take the Cercanías C-4 commuter train from Santa Justa station to Alcalá de Guadaíra (25 min, €1.80, non-folding bikes allowed in designated areas on Cercanías — confirm on the renfe.com or via the Cercanías app before travel; avoid peak commute hours). In Alcalá: the Moorish castle (Castillo de Alcalá, free, open early) overlooks the Guadaira River and is worth 20 minutes before rolling east. The Via Verde de los Alcores departs east from Alcalá and runs 60km to Carmona along a converted railway alignment through the Los Alcores escarpment: a long limestone ridge rising from the Guadalquivir plain, with views south toward the Doñana wetlands and east toward the Sierra. Surface is a mix of compacted gravel and paved sections; 430m cumulative elevation gain makes this the most challenging day but nothing sustained. The route passes through ancient mill sites on the Guadaira (Molino de Algarrobo, Molino de la Aceña) and a spectacular 123-meter dragon bridge structure near Alcalá.
days.afternoon: Arrive in Carmona (~4 hours riding at touring pace). Carmona is a medieval hilltop town of 28,000 people with a disproportionate number of excellent places to eat and an almost complete absence of tourist infrastructure. Lunch at any bar on the Plaza de San Fernando — the local half-ration of salmorejo (a thicker, richer Córdoban version of gazpacho) with jamón ibérico is the correct order. The Roman Necropolis of Carmona (Avenida de Jorge Bonsor, €1.50) is one of the best-preserved Roman burial sites in Spain — family tombs with frescoed interiors cut into the rock. Walk the walls. The Parador of Carmona (an Alcázar converted to a state-run hotel) has a terrace bar with a view over the Guadalquivir plain that is worth buying one drink for.
days.evening: Return to Seville by bus (Damas/Casal buses run Carmona–Seville regularly, ~45 min, €2.50; bikes may not be accepted depending on the coach — check in advance, or arrange a taxi for the final leg). OR: if legs permit, ride back west to Alcalá via the Via Verde and catch the Cercanías back. Final night in Seville: dinner at Bar Eslava if you missed it on Day 2, or anywhere on the Alameda de Hércules (the long tree-lined boulevard north of the historic center) which is the city's outdoor living room on summer evenings — locals dining outside until midnight, impromptu flamenco at the corners, the temperature finally dropping to something manageable.

tips: The heat rule, non-negotiable: Seville in July regularly hits 42°C by noon. All outdoor cycling must happen before 9am or after 6:30pm — full stop. This is not about comfort; it's about safety. Locals have a phrase for it: 'Seville in July, only the mad and the foreigners are outside at noon.' Set an alarm for 5:30am every riding day. The reward is the city at its most beautiful — golden light, empty roads, temperature in the low 20s.

Bike transport logistics: international airlines charge €50–100 each way for bikes as checked baggage in a hard case or properly boxed. Factor this into the trip cost. In Seville, ATD BIKES (Calle Fray Isidoro de Sevilla, near the Plaza de Armas bus station) is a well-regarded repair shop open all day (unusual in Spain, where most shops close 2–6pm) — good to know if you need a tube, cable, or emergency fix. Spanish mechanics are generally excellent and used to working on touring and road bikes.

Water: the route guides mention public water fountains at intervals on the city bike lanes and at village fuentes (street fountains) on the rural routes. Spanish tap water is safe and high quality. Carry two large bottles minimum on any ride leaving the city — the terrain is flat but the heat creates sweat rates you may not be used to. The local 'alimentaciones' (corner stores) in every village stock water, fruit, and snacks and are often open even on Sunday mornings.

Bike on Spanish trains: Cercanías (local/commuter trains) allow non-folding bikes in designated bike carriages for free during off-peak hours; confirm current policy at renfe.com or the Cercanías app. High-speed AVE trains have strict, limited bike rules (usually folded bikes only, or disassembled in a bag). If you're planning the Alcalá day trip by Cercanías, go before 7am or after 9am to avoid the peak crush.

Sherry territory: Seville is 80km from Jerez de la Frontera, the capital of the sherry-producing region — an entirely flat, windswept route through sunflower fields and white villages. If you have a fourth day or want to extend, the ride from Seville to Jerez (or just as far as Lebrija, 60km) and a bodega visit is an obvious extension. Bodegas González Byass (Jerez) and Bodegas Tradición (also Jerez) both accept visitor appointments; the latter is also one of the best private collections of Spanish Golden Age painting in the country.

July timing caveat: most professional cycling guides recommend Seville in October–March for optimal riding. July is when the committed and the heat-adapted go. The cycling infrastructure means city riding is always doable in the early hours; the countryside routes are best with a pre-dawn start. If you're flexible on dates, late September or October would give you all the same routes plus four extra hours of comfortable outdoor window per day.

destination.vibe: The birthplace of democracy, tragedy, and the gyro — where a 2,500-year-old citadel glows gold at sunset over a city of five million, a souvlaki costs €4, and nothing serious happens before 9pm.

flights.outbound_dates: July–August flexible (midweek Tue/Wed cheapest; book 4–6 weeks out)
flights.notes: Connects through Istanbul (IST). Turkish flies 7x/week from DTW. Full-service with meals, 2 checked bags included. Often lowest-priced European carrier on this route.
Connects through JFK or ATL. 12 weekly connecting flights from DTW. Use Google Flights to find cheapest specific dates before booking at delta.com.

flights.outbound_dates: Aug 1–20 (August slightly cheaper than July peak)
flights.notes: Connects through Newark (EWR). EWR→ATH leg is nonstop — slightly shorter overall than Delta's JFK routing.

lodging.name: City Circus Athens
lodging.highlights: 9.6/10 on Hostelworld — neoclassical building with antique furnishings and retro character, steps from Monastiraki Metro and Psirri's bar scene. 2026 Hoscar Award winner.

lodging.name: Sweet Home Hotel
lodging.highlights: 9.3/10 on Booking (1,600 reviews) — elegant rooms 400m from Syntagma Square, some with Acropolis views, homemade breakfast included.

lodging.name: AthensWas Design Hotel
lodging.highlights: 21 rooms on the pedestrian promenade directly below the Acropolis with unobstructed Parthenon views from rooms and rooftop. Greek contemporary design throughout.

itinerary.overview: Athens is not a museum with a city around it — it's a city of five million that happens to have an intact 5th-century BC temple on its central hill. The Acropolis is genuinely awe-inspiring and you should see it, but the city underneath it rewards the visitor who keeps walking: Psirri's tavernas where Greek blues (rebetiko) still gets performed live, the Ancient Agora where democracy was argued over in real time, Exarcheia's anarchist bookstores and Saturday farmers' market, the Kerameikos cemetery that has been burying Athenians since the 11th century BC.

days.title: Acropolis at dawn, Anafiotika, Ancient Agora, rebetiko by night
days.morning: Book your Acropolis tickets in advance at odysseus.culture.gr (€30, time slots required). Go at 8am opening — before the heat peaks and the tour groups arrive. After the main ruins, walk the north slope to Anafiotika: a whitewashed island-village of 45 tiny houses built in the 1840s by craftsmen from the island of Anafi who exploited a legal clause allowing overnight construction. Blue doors, bougainvillea, cats, stone steps too narrow for a moped, zero tour buses.
days.afternoon: Walk to the Ancient Agora — the actual civic and commercial heart of ancient Athens, less famous and far less crowded than the Acropolis. The Temple of Hephaestus here is better preserved than the Parthenon and gives a clearer sense of what a working Greek temple looked like. Then the Acropolis Museum for the afternoon: climate-controlled, with the Caryatid porch sculptures and glass floors above the archaeological dig below.
days.evening: Dinner in Psirri — Koudounaki (family-run, two sisters, handwritten menu, €15–20 for a full meal) or Enastron for classic mezedes. After dinner: Stoa Athanaton for rebetiko, Athens' Greek blues, live and authentic, usually starting after 10pm.

days.title: Kerameikos, Varvakios Market, Exarcheia, rooftop Acropolis view
days.morning: Varvakios Central Market at 8am — operating since 1884, marble counters, whole animals, breakfast soup counters inside. Then 10 minutes west to Kerameikos — the ancient cemetery of Athens, where the city has been burying its dead since the 11th century BC. Quiet, undervisited, and the on-site museum holds extraordinary funerary relief portraits. Entrance €8.
days.afternoon: Walk north toward Exarcheia — Athens' self-declared anarchist neighborhood, bookshops, murals, and on Saturdays the Kallidromiou Street Farmers' Market. Stop at Gamay wine bar for Greek low-intervention wines.
days.evening: Monastiraki for the night shot: A for Athens rooftop bar (6th floor, Miaouli 2) with unobstructed Parthenon views after dark. Then Cine Thission outdoor cinema where films play against the illuminated Acropolis backdrop.

days.title: Cape Sounion day trip, Dionysiou Areopagitou walk, Koukaki dinner
days.morning: Early KTEL Attica bus to Cape Sounion (70km south, 1.5–2h, €7 each way). Temple of Poseidon sits 65 meters above the Aegean on a limestone cliff — columns intact, Byron's name carved into one of them. Arrive before 10am.
days.afternoon: Back in Athens by 2pm. Walk Dionysiou Areopagitou — the pedestrian promenade wrapping the Acropolis base, described as one of the most beautiful walks in Europe. Climb Philopappos Hill for the quiet, crowd-free Parthenon view.
days.evening: Dinner in Koukaki, south of the Acropolis Museum — local prices, chalkboard menus. To Steki Tou Ilia (Thissio) for grilled lamb chops sold by weight and fried potatoes that are also the point.

tips: Book Acropolis at odysseus.culture.gr (€30 combined ticket includes Agora, Kerameikos, and other sites). Go at 8am or two hours before sunset — never midday in July.
Order a freddo espresso immediately upon sitting anywhere. It's espresso shaken with ice until frothy, €3.50, and the only coffee that makes sense in an Athenian July.
Always choose EUROS at ATMs and payment terminals, never your home currency. Dynamic currency conversion is a legal hidden 5–10% fee.
Rebetiko is Greece's blues and it's still performed live — Stoa Athanaton (30 years running) and Palia Markiza (since 1963, Fri–Sun). Neither starts before 10pm.
Anafiotika is on the north slope of the Acropolis above Plaka — walk uphill on any narrow path from Plaka square. 45 tiny houses built with a legal loophole, no tour buses, genuinely not touristic.
The Acropolis Museum (base of hill) and the Acropolis hill are separate things. Do the hill first, museum second.

sources.title: The Ultimate Athens Travel Guide (2026 Edition) — Travel the Greek Way
2026 Athens Travel Guide: Explore Greece's Capital Like a Local — Greeking.me
10 Unique Things to Do in Athens 2026 — Julia's Days Off
Monastiraki and Psirri Travel Guide — Fodor's Travel

destination.vibe: The city that taught Europe to eat — 900 years of university life, the world's longest portico, tagliatelle al ragù that has nothing to do with 'spaghetti bolognese,' and terracotta buildings so red they named a political color after them.

lodging.name: Ostello Bello Bologna
lodging.highlights: 9.5/10 on Hostelworld — free daily events, multilingual staff, unlimited coffee, 10 min walk from Piazza Maggiore.

lodging.name: Art Hotel Orologio
lodging.highlights: 24 rooms literally on Piazza Maggiore, free breakfast, free bike rental, 19th-century building.

lodging.name: Art Hotel Commercianti
lodging.highlights: A 12th-century former town hall with vaulted ceilings and windows onto the Basilica San Petronio's Gothic stained glass.

itinerary.overview: Bologna is La Grassa, La Dotta, La Rossa — the fat, the learned, the red. Home to the oldest university in the Western world (est. 1088), 38 miles of UNESCO porticos, and the cooking that the rest of Italian food is trying to be.

destination.vibe: The city that went from narco capital to Most Innovative City on Earth in one generation — and is now doing it again with food, coffee, street art, and a metro system that's become a point of civic pride.
No nonstops, no problem — Bangkok rewards the extra hours in transit with 12 million people, temples against skyscrapers, Michelin street food for $1, and the baht weaker than it's been in years.
Twenty-two million people, the world's best street tacos, Diego Rivera murals on government buildings, Frida's blue house, Aztec ruins under a baroque cathedral, mezcal bars in converted mansions.

Seven hills, a 1755 earthquake that flattened everything and got rebuilt fast, 600 years of seafaring bravado, and the most melancholic pop music genre ever invented — all held together by custard tarts and cheap wine.
A city that does everything well and brags about none of it — temples next to bubble tea next to Michelin stalls next to the world's best convenience stores.
Ten million people, 600 years of history, and the world's most obsessive food culture — all running at 5G.
